variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill (Aryamehr / National Iranian Steel Corporation) — Site selection
notes: Isfahan chosen; availability of water was the prime consideration, plus central location relative to raw materials (Kerman-area coal/iron ore) and markets (concentrated in Tehran area)

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Financing
notes: Financed via a gas-sales arrangement (barter) with the Soviet Union -- natural gas exports as repayment, attractive partly because no alternative export market for the gas existed at the time (LNG liquefaction not yet commercially developed)

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Supplier selection context
notes: USSR was reportedly the ONLY country prepared to supply an integrated iron and steelworks to Iran at the time of planning, limiting the Government's choice

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Associated town
notes: Aryashar -- a new modern town built alongside the steel complex to serve it; construction cost of the town was NOT initially separated from the steel plant's cost in official accounting

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Stage 1 capacity/target date
notes: 750,000 tons of ingot steel by mid-1972 (planned output later limited to this figure rather than a more economically efficient 1.2 million tons, due to a binding constraint: limited supply of high-quality coking coal and water for coal washeries at the mines -- required somewhat uneconomic coal-mining procedures to hit the startup date)

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Stage 2 capacity/target date
notes: 2 to 2.5 million tons by 1975

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Stage 3 capacity/target date
notes: 4 to 5 million tons by the end of the 1970s

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Site infrastructure ultimate capacity
notes: Infrastructure at Isfahan estimated adequate for a mill of up to 7 million tons, though planning beyond 4-5 million tons was 'not yet advanced' as of this 1972 report

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Equipment
notes: Conventional blast furnace with gas fuel injection, coke ovens and auxiliary by-product plant, L.D. (Linz-Donawitz) steel converters, continuous casting equipment, and rolling mills; bars/rods (light to heavy sections) rolled up to 2 million ton capacity, flat-rolled products envisaged only once capacity exceeds 2 million tons

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Construction status (as of 1972 report)
notes: Construction running according to schedule -- described as 'a formidable achievement' given enterprise size, attributed to successful collaboration between Iranian civil engineers in charge and their Russian technical advisers

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Investment cost assessment
notes: Report states it was 'impossible accurately to assess the investment' for the first-stage plant specifically, because much of the initial investment was already made in preparation for the larger 2-million-plus-ton stages, and infrastructure/town costs were commingled with plant costs in the accounts -- no single all-in dollar figure given in this source

variant_label: Isfahan Steel Mill — Competitive positioning risk noted by report
notes: Report flags that the Isfahan mill's initial product mix (bars/rods) would compete directly with existing private bar-rolling mills near Ahwaz, and notes it was 'perhaps unfortunate' the Isfahan mill did not begin with flat-rolled products given larger unmet demand for those

variant_label: IDRO (Industrial Development and Renovation Organization) capital-goods plants — Aluminum plant
notes: Location: Arak. Partner: Reynolds (US) -- matches the 'Iralco' aluminum smelter also documented independently in the USGS Minerals Yearbook 1975 chapter (same round, see usgs-minerals-yearbook/historical-pre1990-volumes/iran-production-extraction/)

variant_label: IDRO capital-goods plants — Heavy engineering complex
notes: Location: Arak. Same financing arrangement as the steel complex (i.e. USSR barter financing)

variant_label: IDRO capital-goods plants — Machine tool factory
notes: Location: Tabriz. Financed and technically assisted by Czechoslovakia

variant_label: IDRO capital-goods plants — Agricultural equipment factory
notes: Location: Tabriz. Financed and technically assisted by Romania

variant_label: IDRO capital-goods plants — Common characteristics noted by report
notes: All (except the aluminum plant) described as 'very solid construction' but 'lacking in flexibility'; all being completed on schedule and locally managed; report flags a forward risk that combined production capacity of these plants will exceed local product demand for some time, making IDRO's product-mix design and export strategy important for economic multi-shift utilization

variant_label: Textile sector (mass-consumption segment) — Price competitiveness
notes: 'Chit' and 'khodari' mass-consumption textiles -- at least 70 percent of total domestic textile consumption -- priced approximately 25-40 percent above the average price of similar products imported into the USA (net of indirect taxes); wool prices (net of high import duties on wool) roughly comparable to import prices

variant_label: Automotive sector — Paykan price competitiveness
notes: Selected automobile makes in Iran priced 15-40 percent above international prices; the Paykan specifically -- described as having 'the largest market' -- was only about 15 percent above the price set by a recently-determined international competitive tender in Kuwait, i.e. the most internationally price-competitive Iranian-made car of the ones compared

variant_label: Automotive sector — Buses
notes: Roughly comparable in price to imports, attributed to the high proportion of labor cost in bus production

variant_label: Automotive sector — Forward planning (1972)
notes: Import substitution being extended to motor-vehicle COMPONENT industries specifically (as opposed to just final assembly) -- new intermediate-product plants planned/underway for piston, gearbox and electrical-systems production to service the motor vehicle industry; a compressor plant likewise planned to service the refrigerator industry

variant_label: Capital goods sector (national context) — Composition (1972)
notes: Capital goods production 'presently very small' -- chief components: trucks, buses, trailers, communications equipment, and water pumps. New IDRO-linked public enterprises were adding machine tools, agricultural machinery and other engineering products
